titleEn: The Traces of Dhat al-Asabi' Are Effaced

verses.ajuz: إذا ما كان مغث أو لحاء

verses.sadr: لنا في كل يوم من معد

translationEn: The traces of Dhat al-Asabi' to al-Jiwa' are effaced, up to Adhra' whose dwelling lies empty. Dwellings of the Banu al-Hashhas, desolate, swept by dust-storms and rain from the sky. There used to be people dwelling there always, amid their meadows with cattle and sheep. But leave this — rather, who is it that gently keeps me awake when evening falls? It is Sha'tha' who has enslaved his heart, and there is no cure for his heart from her. As if a wine from Bayt Ra's, its blend mixed with honey and water, were upon her teeth, or the taste of fresh apples bent down from the branch. When the wine-cups are mentioned one day, they are ransom for the sweetness of the wine. We blame the wine if we suffer, whenever there is distress or contention. We drink it and it leaves us as kings and as lions whom battle cannot restrain. May we lose our horses if you do not see them raising dust-clouds, their meeting-place at Kada'! They race the bridles going upward, upon their shoulders the thirsty spears. Our steeds remain drenched in sweat while the women slap them with their veils. If you turn away from us, we shall enter in pilgrimage and the conquest will come and the veil will be lifted. Otherwise, endure a day of fierce combat in which God grants might to whom He wills. Gabriel, God's trusted one, is among us, and the Holy Spirit has no equal. God said: I have sent a servant who speaks the truth, even if trials come. I bore witness to him — so stand up and believe him! But you said: We shall not stand, nor do we wish to. God said: I have prepared an army — they are the Ansar, whose purpose is battle. Every day we face from the tribe of Ma'add insults, or fighting, or satire. We judge with our verses whoever satirizes us, and we strike when blood is mingled. Convey to Abu Sufyan from me: you are hollow, empty, full of air. You satirized Muhammad, so I answered on his behalf, and with God lies the recompense. Do you mock him when you are not his equal? The worse of you two is ransom for the better. You mocked one who is blessed, righteous, a true believer — God's trustee whose nature is faithfulness. Whoever among you mocks the Messenger of God — praising him and supporting him are one and the same. My father, his father, and my honor are a shield for the honor of Muhammad against you. If the Banu Lu'ayy catch the Judhayma — killing them is a cure. Those are a people who aided against us, yet in our claws is their blood. The alliance of al-Harith ibn Abi Dirar and the alliance of Qurayza — we are free of them. My tongue is a sharp blade without flaw, and my sea cannot be muddied by any bucket.
context: Hassan's most famous and longest qasida, combining the traditional pre-Islamic nasib (nostalgic desert prelude) with a fierce defense of Islam and the Prophet. It addresses Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith directly, and contains the celebrated closing verse about Hassan's tongue being an unblemished sword. The Prophet reportedly said: 'Hassan's words wound the Quraysh more than arrows.'

titleAr: وأحسن منك لم تر قط عيني
titleEn: More Beautiful Than You No Eye Has Seen

verses.sadr: وأحسن منك لم تر قط عيني

translationEn: More beautiful than you no eye has ever seen, and more handsome than you no woman has ever borne. You were created free from every flaw, as though you were created exactly as you wished.
context: This is perhaps the most widely memorized couplet in all of Arabic prophetic praise poetry (madih nabawi). Hassan composed it as a compact, jewel-like tribute to the Prophet Muhammad. Its concise perfection made it a staple of Islamic devotional gatherings for over fourteen centuries.

titleEn: Does a Faded Trace of the Dwelling Answer?

verses.sadr: واشك الهموم إلى الإله وما ترى
من بعد ما قنطوا ، ففرج عنهم

translationEn: Does a faded, desolate trace of the dwelling respond to questions with an answer? I once saw its inhabitants adorned — bright of face, illustrious in lineage. But leave the dwellings and the memory of every secluded, fair, gentle-spoken maiden. Complain your sorrows to God, and what you see of a people banded together in wrath. They aimed their raid at the Messenger, and they roused the villages and the Bedouin nomads. An army with Uyayna and Ibn Harb among them, raging with the muster of the Confederates. Until they reached Medina, hoping for the Prophet's death and the spoils of war. They came upon us, powerful in their hands, yet they were driven back in their fury on their heels. By the blowing of a fierce storm that scattered their host, and the soldiers of your Lord, Master of all lords. God spared the believers their fighting and rewarded them with the best of rewards. After they had despaired, He relieved them — a revelation from our Sovereign, the Bestower. He gladdened the eyes of Muhammad and his companions, and humiliated every denier and doubter. One who wears disbelief beneath his garments — and disbelief does not make garments pure. Wretchedness clung to his heart, and it showed him the path of disbelief to the end of these ages.
context: This qasida commemorates the Battle of the Trench (al-Khandaq/al-Ahzab, 627 CE), when a coalition of Quraysh, Ghatafan, and other tribes besieged Medina. The poem describes how God scattered the Confederate army with a fierce wind and invisible forces, echoing Quran 33:9. It is a major example of Hassan's war poetry blending historical narrative with Quranic allusion.

titleEn: I Recognized Zaynab's Dwelling at the Sand-Dune
translationEn: I recognized Zaynab's dwelling at the sand-dune, like the script of revelation on fresh parchment. Winds assailed it, and every dark rain-cloud of the early season poured down upon it. Its traces became worn, and it lay desolate after its beloved inhabitants departed. Leave off this remembrance each day, and cool the burning of your grieving breast. Tell truthfully, without the report of a liar, of what the Sovereign wrought on the morning of Badr — what share He gave us against the polytheists. A morning when their host seemed like Mount Hira' whose pillars appeared at twilight. We met them with a host of ours like lions of the thicket — beardless youths and grey-haired men. Before Muhammad, they rallied around him against the enemies in the heat of wars. In their hands were sharp, polished swords, and every tested lance with bulging knots. The noble Aws, supported by the Banu al-Najjar, in steadfast faith. We left Abu Jahl struck down, and Utba we left in the dust. And Shayba we left among men of noble lineage. The Messenger of God called out to them when we cast them in heaps into the well: Did you not find my words to be true? God's command seizes the hearts. They did not speak, but had they spoken they would have said: You spoke the truth, and yours was a sound judgment.
context: A major qasida on the Battle of Badr (624 CE), the first decisive Muslim victory. Hassan names specific Quraysh leaders killed — Abu Jahl, Utba, and Shayba — and describes the famous scene where the Prophet addressed the dead cast into the well of Badr, asking if they now found God's promise to be true. This scene is also recorded in the hadith collections.

titleEn: My Night at Juman Grew Long

verses.ajuz: بها لا أريد النوم حتى تغيبا

verses.sadr: إذا غار منها كوكب بعد كوكب

verses.ajuz: وصرف النوى من أن تشت وتشعبا

verses.sadr: وفي الطير بالعلياء إذ عرضت لنا ،
verses.ajuz: وما الطير إلا أن تمر وتنعبا

verses.sadr: وقد بان ما يأتي من الأمر ، واكتست
أتجمع شوقا إن تراخت بها النوى

verses.ajuz: عصا البين لم تسطع لشعثاء مطلبا

verses.sadr: وكيف تصدي المرء ذي اللب للصبا ،
ألا لا أرى جارا يعلل نفسه

translationEn: My night at Juman grew long, and the leading stars showed no sign of setting. I stayed awake watching them as though appointed their guardian, not wanting sleep until they disappeared. As star after star sank, my eye watched at the end of the night for another star. Setting stars following one another — you would think them, at dawn's approach, weary crawling creatures. I feared the sudden surprise of separation, and the turning of distance that scatters and divides. I became certain when the tribe struck their tents that the shocks of parting would turn the head grey. The eloquent caller announced to you a departure, as the afternoon sun inclined to set. The crow's cry made clear their estrangement, the evening it perched atop a ban tree and sang. And in the birds above when they appeared to us — birds that are nothing but passing and croaking. On the morning of departure, love nearly overwhelmed me; I struggled with myself to rise and mount. How can one not forget youthful passions after passing forty and gaining experience? What is coming is clear, and the temples have taken on a strange hue of grey. Do you gather longing when distance stretches between you, yet turn away coldly when she draws near? When the ties of love are severed and the staff of parting is broken, you can find no way to reach Sha'tha'. How can a man of sense pursue youthful love when he has no excuse if he acts frivolously? I keep my distance from them — not from hatred, but out of caution, respect, and companionship. Alas, I see no neighbor who flatters himself obeyed, nor any neighbor reproached on Sha'tha's behalf.
context: A pre-Islamic ghazal (love poem) from Hassan's Jahili period, before his conversion to Islam. It follows the classical nasib structure: a sleepless night watching the stars, fear of the beloved tribe's departure, the ominous crow, and the poet's self-reproach for lingering in youthful love past the age of forty. This reveals Hassan's mastery of the traditional qasida form.

titleAr: إن تمس دار ابن أروى منه خالية
titleEn: If the House of Ibn Arwa Lies Empty

verses.sadr: إن تمس دار ابن أروى منه خالية
يا أيها الناس أبدوا ذات أنفسكم ،
verses.ajuz: لا يستوي الصدق عند الله والكذب
مستلئما قد بدا في وجهه الغضب

translationEn: If the house of Ibn Arwa lies empty of him — a gate fallen, a gate broken, in ruin — yet the seeker of good would find his need in it, and to it would come renown and noble lineage. O people, reveal what is in your souls! Truthfulness and falsehood are not equal before God. Unless you repent and turn to God's command, you will face a raid of bands, one after another. Among them is Habib, the blazing star of war, who leads them armored, his face showing fury.
context: A poem blending praise and warning, addressing the people about the emptied house of Ibn Arwa (Uthman ibn Affan's mother was Arwa). Hassan calls people to truthfulness before God and warns of the Muslim warriors ready for battle, led by a fierce commander.

titleEn: What Did You Resent of Worn Garments?
translationEn: What did you resent — worn garments, servants, slave-women, and gold? You said 'change!' — and so He replaced you with a scorching year of drought and a war like fire. One group perishing from emaciation, another already destroyed and gone. When you killed a glorious man of firm resolve, of clear practice and well-known lineage.
context: A sharp satirical poem (hija') mocking enemies who rejected Islam's simple way of life. Hassan reminds them that their complaint about the Muslims' modest appearance was answered by God with drought and war. The reference to killing a 'glorious man of known lineage' likely refers to one of the early Muslim martyrs.

titleEn: When the Adal Were Driven to Us
translationEn: When the Adal were driven to us, they were like a snared gazelle with marked brows. We set upon you with devastating, punishing thrusts, and we surrounded you with blows from every side. Were it not for the banner of al-Harithiyya, they would have been sold in the markets like imported slaves. They suck the bases of arrows as though, when they descend into the plain, they were lean, emaciated camels. We drive people away from us until it is as though burning coals of piercing fire scorch them.
context: A poem of tribal pride (fakhr) boasting about military victory over the Adal tribe. Hassan describes the enemy's humiliation in vivid terms — reduced to sucking arrow-shafts and nearly sold as slaves. The poem showcases the intense martial imagery characteristic of Hassan's war poetry.

titleEn: God's Blessings on Those Who Fell in Succession
translationEn: God's blessings upon those who fell in succession on the day of al-Raji' — they were honored and rewarded. At the head of the band was Marthad, and their commander was the son of al-Bukayr before them, and Khubayb. And the son of Tariq, and Ibn Dathina among them — his appointed death came to him there. He refused to be led captive, that they might take his back, and fought on — truly he was noble. And Asim, killed at their Raji', earned glory — indeed he was one who earned it well.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fell in succession, followed one another

context: An elegy (ritha') for the Muslim martyrs of the Expedition of al-Raji' (625 CE), when a group of Muslim missionaries were betrayed and killed or captured by the Hudhayl tribe. Hassan commemorates specific companions by name: Marthad, Khubayb ibn Adi, and Asim ibn Thabit, whose bodies were reportedly protected by wasps from mutilation.

titleEn: I Swore an Oath That Was No Lie

verses.ajuz: لا يغبقون من المعزى ، إذا آبوا

verses.sadr: كانوا إذا حضروا شيب العقار لهم ،
إذا لآبوا جميعا ، أو لكان لهم
verses.ajuz: أسرى من القوم أو قتلى وأسلاب
ليس لهم عند يوم البأس أحساب

translationEn: I swore an oath that was no lie: if al-Harith al-Ghassani had companions from the root-stock of Ghassan, with their sword-belts hanging loose, who do not milk goats when they return — men not driven away, their eyes reddened, when they come to the noble man's door — men who, when they attended, had aged wine, and cups and goblets were passed among them — then they would have returned together, or they would have taken prisoners, slain, and spoils. They would have fought wherever death overtook them, until they won back their captives and spoils. But he only met with a rabble who had no standing on the day of valor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rabble, mixed crowd of low status

context: Hassan praises the Ghassanid king al-Harith while lamenting that he was accompanied by unworthy fighters rather than true Ghassanid warriors. The poem reflects Hassan's pre-Islamic connection to the Ghassanid court, where he was a court poet before Islam. The Ghassanids were an Arab Christian kingdom on the Byzantine frontier.

titleEn: She Said to Him One Day, Speaking to Him
translationEn: She said to him one day, addressing him — full-hipped, a slender-waisted maiden: 'As for handsomeness and chivalry, or the judgment of men — that much is evident; enough for me. But I wish you would tell us: who are your parents, and what is your tribal branch?' I laughed, then raised my voice in a continuous stream at the time of boisterous speech: 'My grandfather is Abu Layla, and his father is Amr, and my maternal uncles are the Banu Ka'b. I am of the people who, when winter bites hard as the ally of drought, their wealthy give to their needy, and who strike hard in places of terror.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bit hard, gripped (metaphor for severe winter)

context: A charming fakhr (boasting) poem cast as a dialogue. A beautiful woman tests Hassan by asking about his lineage. He responds with proud genealogy and tribal virtues — generosity in hard times and bravery in battle. This poetic form of lineage boasting through a woman's questioning is a common motif in pre-Islamic Arab poetry.

titleEn: The Place of Adhib Has Faded After Us

verses.sadr: فابك ما شئت على ما انقضى ،

translationEn: The place of Adhib has faded after us — none dwelling there, none drawing near. The wind altered it, blowing dust upon it, and the roar of its ceaseless thunder. There once dwelled in it a tender, full-figured, budding maiden. She entrusted my heart with her memory, and love is for me a crushing, overpowering force. There is no consolation for me from her, and there is no escaping what fate brings. When I remember her, it is as though I have drunk the heady wine. Is the plateau of Dhi Nafar as I knew it, then the bends of al-A'raf, then al-Darib? And the bends of al-Kharba — when our people, every evening, gathered in talk and play? Weep as you wish over what has passed — every bond is a vanishing, departing thing. If tears could restore anything, your flowing tears would have restored it. Su'da was never going to be fair to me — rarely does the companion treat me fairly. Like a brother of mine whom I do not reproach, though what the reproacher would demand is great. The one present reported his words — that which the absent one conceals from us. And there appeared from him one wrapped up, whose forbearance was lost in her folly.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who stays up talking at night

context: A melancholy ghazal following the classical nasib pattern: the effaced campsite, memory of the beloved, the inevitability of separation. The philosophical verse 'every bond is a vanishing, departing thing' and the observation that tears cannot restore the past mark this as a mature reflection on love and loss from Hassan's pre-Islamic period.

titleEn: Then By God We Shall Hurl War Upon Them
translationEn: Then, by God, we shall hurl upon them a war that turns the child grey before old age.
context: A single devastating verse — a rajaz-like war cry. The hyperbolic image of a war so terrible it turns children's hair grey before their time is a classic trope of Arabic threat poetry. Such isolated powerful verses (mufradat) were often quoted as proverbs and battle cries.

titleEn: Fayruz Bereaved Us — May He Have No Worth

verses.sadr: رؤوف على الأدنى ، غليظ على العدا
متى ما يقل لا يكذب القول فعله

translationEn: Fayruz bereaved us — may he have no worth — of a radiant man who recited the firm verses of the Quran, turning to God in repentance. Gentle toward those close to him, stern against enemies, a trusted brother in times of calamity, noble. Whenever he spoke, his deeds never belied his words — swift to do good, never frowning.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bereaved us, afflicted us with loss

context: An elegy for a companion murdered by Fayruz (Abu Lu'lu'a al-Firuz), likely referring to the assassination of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab in 644 CE. Hassan describes the victim as one who recited the Quran, was compassionate to the near and stern to enemies — echoing Quranic descriptions of the Prophet's companions.

titleEn: We Were Absent from the Plain of Mecca

verses.sadr: فيا ليت شعري ! هل تنالن نصرتي
وصفوان عودا حز من شفر استه
فلا تأمننا ، يا ابن أم مجالد

translationEn: We were absent and you did not witness at the plain of Mecca the men of Banu Ka'b having their necks severed — at the hands of men who did not draw their swords in justice, and slain ones whose garments were not soiled in guilt. Would that I knew whether my aid shall reach Suhayl ibn Amr — the shame and punishment of it! And Safwan — may a stick slash from the edge of his backside. Now is the time of war; its headband is tied tight. Do not feel safe from us, O son of Umm Mujalid, when war has conceived and its fang has grown crooked. Had a band of us been present at the plain, the fighting that day would have been easy for us.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: conceived (war metaphor: war is pregnant with violence)

context: Hassan laments being absent from Mecca when the Banu Ka'b were attacked unjustly. He names Quraysh leaders Suhayl ibn Amr and Safwan ibn Umayya, threatening them with retribution. The poem's martial rhetoric and personal naming reflect the tradition of tribal war poetry that Hassan brought into the service of Islam.

titleAr: يا حار ، قد عولت ، غير معول ،
titleEn: O Har, You Relied on What Cannot Be Relied Upon

verses.sadr: يا حار ، قد عولت ، غير معول ،
هلا عطفت على ابن أمك إذ ثوى

translationEn: O Har, you relied on what cannot be relied upon at the time of tumult and the hour of reckoning. When you mounted a fine she-camel, swift-running and light-flanked, riding hard. While the men behind you — you abandoned their fight, hoping for escape, but it was no time for fleeing. Why did you not turn back for your brother when he fell, pierced by spear-points, his spoils left unclaimed? By your life, Jahm, had you been struck by the like of it, Akhtham would have come to you, baring his fangs. The Sovereign hastened for him and destroyed his host with shameful disgrace and evil punishment. Had you been the offspring of a noble woman, you would have acquitted yourself well — but you are the spawn of the daughter of Uqab.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you relied upon, you depended on

context: A biting satire against al-Harith (Har), mocking him for fleeing battle while his kinsman was killed. Hassan employs the classic hija' technique of questioning the enemy's lineage and mother's honor, concluding that cowardice is hereditary. The poem likely dates to one of the early Islamic battles.

titleEn: When Quraysh Traces Its Lineage, They Reject You

verses.sadr: وإن التي ألقتك من تحت رجلها ،

translationEn: When Quraysh traces its lineage one day, they reject you; but if the tribe of Shuja' claims kinship, you are their peer. The one who cast you from beneath her foot as an infant — her milk was the milk of baseness. Your mother is from Qasr, Habasha was her mother — a dark woman of Fahm whose perfume is stale urine.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: her peer, her match in lineage

context: A harsh genealogical satire (hija') attacking an enemy's lineage by claiming that the prestigious Quraysh tribe disowns him. Lineage attacks were the most devastating weapon in the Arabic poetic arsenal — the claim that one's blood is impure or that one's mother was of low origin struck at the core of tribal Arab identity.

titleAr: يا حار قد كنت لولا ما رميت به ،
titleEn: O Har, You Would Have Been — Were It Not for What You Did

verses.sadr: يا حار قد كنت لولا ما رميت به ،
verses.ajuz: لله درك ، في عز وفي حسب
ما لم يجلله حي من العرب

verses.sadr: يا سالب البيت ذي الأركان حليته

verses.ajuz: أين الغزال عليه الدر من ذهب ؟
تبا لذلك من شيخ ومن عقب

translationEn: O Har, were it not for what you are accused of, by God, you would have had might and noble lineage. You have draped your people in disgrace and shame that no Arab tribe has ever worn. O you who robbed the Ka'ba of its ornament — return the gazelle, for what is stolen cannot be hidden! Ask the sons of al-Harith who humiliates his people: where is the gazelle adorned with pearls and gold? Wretched are the sons and wretched is their elder — shame upon that elder and his posterity!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the gazelle (a golden ornament of the Ka'ba)

context: Hassan attacks a man who stole the famous golden gazelle ornament from the Ka'ba. Theft from the Ka'ba was among the gravest offenses in pre-Islamic and Islamic Arabia. The poem uses the address to 'Har' (al-Harith) and the demand to return the stolen ornament as a framework for a devastating attack on his entire lineage.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك منسكب ،
titleEn: O Eye, Pour Forth Your Flowing Tears

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك منسكب ،
verses.ajuz: وابكي خبيبا مع الغادين لم يؤب

verses.sadr: قد هاج عيني على علات عبرتها ،
verses.ajuz: إذ قيل نص على جذع من الخشب

verses.sadr: بني فكيهة ، إن الحرب قد لقحت

translationEn: O eye, pour forth your flowing tears, and weep for Khubayb who went with those who departed and did not return. A falcon whose station was central among the Ansar, sweet of nature, pure and unmixed. My eye was stirred despite its usual restraint when it was said he was raised upon a wooden trunk. O rider heading off on your journey, convey from me a threat that is no lie. O Banu Fukayha, war has conceived and its milk is bitter poison, being milked for the milker. In it are the lions of Banu al-Najjar who lead them forward — blazing spear-points in a thundering host.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tight, gathered army; a dense host

context: An elegy for Khubayb ibn Adi, one of the most celebrated early Muslim martyrs. Khubayb was captured at al-Raji' and crucified by the Quraysh in Mecca. The image of him 'raised upon a wooden trunk' refers to his crucifixion. Hassan mourns him while threatening retribution from the warriors of Banu al-Najjar.

titleEn: Baseness Built a House Upon Madhhij
translationEn: Baseness built a house upon Madhhij, and it stood fixed over Madhhij. If all the glory that Madhhij possessed were gathered, it would not weigh down a hare.
context: A concise satirical attack on the Madhhij tribal confederation. The metaphor of 'baseness building a house upon them' personifies dishonor as their permanent dwelling, while the punchline — that all their glory would not outweigh a hare — is a classic example of the devastating brevity that made Hassan's satire feared.

titleEn: Who Will Inform Safwan That His Old Woman
translationEn: Who will inform Safwan that his old woman is a slave-woman to the neighbor of Ma'mar ibn Habib? A slave-woman said to be from the Barajim in origin — a lineage far removed from any noble descent. Were it not for the journey and the distance across a desolate waste, I would have left her crawling on her hamstrings.
context: A satirical attack on Safwan ibn Umayya, a prominent Quraysh leader who was among Islam's fiercest opponents before the Conquest of Mecca. Hassan attacks his wife's lineage as slave-born, a devastating insult in tribal Arab society. Safwan later converted to Islam after the Conquest.

titleEn: By God, Hudhayl Does Not Know
translationEn: By God, Hudhayl does not know whether the water of Zamzam is pure or mixed. They have no share, when they perform umrah and hajj, of the two sacred stones or the sa'y. Rather, al-Raji' is their abode, where clear baseness and defects dwell. They deceived Khubayb by their pledge of protection — what a wretched, lying covenant theirs was! You herd them and drive them against me, yet they have lived and have no hearts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two sacred stones (the Black Stone and Maqam Ibrahim)
the sa'y (running between Safa and Marwa)
al-Raji' (site of the treachery against Khubayb)
their pledge of protection, their covenant

context: A fierce satire against the Hudhayl tribe for their treachery at al-Raji' (625 CE), where they betrayed Khubayb ibn Adi and other Muslim missionaries after granting them safe conduct. Hassan strips them of any claim to the sacred rites of Mecca, declaring them unworthy of Zamzam, the Ka'ba stones, and the hajj rituals.

titleEn: Muzayna — No Orator Is Seen Among Them
translationEn: Muzayna — no orator is seen among them, nor fertile land frequented for its abundance. Nor anyone who fills the wooden bowls and defends, when the cold drives the dog into its den. Men among whom good deeds are wasted — they consider a billy-goat the equal of a noble horse.
context: A satirical attack on the Muzayna tribe, mocking them for lacking the three pillars of tribal honor: eloquence (no orators), generosity (no one fills the feast-bowls), and martial nobility (they mistake goats for horses). The final image became proverbial in Arabic literature for mistaking the worthless for the valuable.

titleEn: When Quraysh Is Counted or Registered
translationEn: When Quraysh is counted by lineage or registered, you have no share in its root-stock. The sons of Husays rejected you from their father's line, to Shuja' where water-skins are stolen. And you, O son of al-Mughira, are a slave of Shawl — the rope of your shoulder has already frayed the water-skin. When the noble branches of Quraysh are counted, dogs block the path before your lineage. As for Imran ibn Makhzum, leave that aside — there lie the secret and the purest nobility.
context: A genealogical satire targeting a member of the Makhzum clan of Quraysh, denying his claim to noble Qurayshi lineage. Hassan skilfully differentiates between the genuine nobility of Imran ibn Makhzum and the spurious claims of his target, using the classic hija' technique of splitting a tribe's reputation to isolate the victim.

titleEn: Hudhayl Asked the Messenger of God an Obscenity

verses.ajuz: ضلت هذيل بما جاءت ولم تصب

translationEn: Hudhayl asked the Messenger of God an obscene thing — Hudhayl went astray in what they brought and did not hit the mark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they did not hit the mark, they failed

context: A single devastating verse condemning the Hudhayl tribe for making an outrageous request to the Prophet Muhammad. This terse, epigrammatic style was highly effective — a single well-aimed couplet could destroy a tribe's reputation more thoroughly than a long poem.

titleAr: يا حار إن كنت أمرأ متوسعا
titleEn: O Har, If You Are a Man of Means

verses.sadr: يا حار إن كنت أمرأ متوسعا
أجمعت أنك أنت ألأم من مشى
وأبان لؤمك أن أمك لم تكن

translationEn: O Har, if you are a man of means, then ransom those women who do justice to the family of Janab. Your mother's sisters — you know their situation, and the truth is understood by people of sense. Al-Farafisa ibn al-Ahwas holds captive your mother's kin, daughters of Uqab. I am convinced that you are the basest man who ever walked — with the lewdness of a harlot and the vanity of a crow. Thus your ancestors bequeathed you — they departed and you were left in disgrace and torment. You inherited from your father treachery and depravity, and baseness when lineages are measured. Your vileness made it clear that your mother was nothing but a mate for the worst of the Bedouin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who consorts with lowly people

context: Another poem in Hassan's sustained satirical campaign against al-Harith ibn Hisham (Har). Here Hassan mocks him for failing to ransom his own female relatives from captivity — a grave failure of tribal duty. The accusation that one's mother was of low Bedouin stock and that vileness is inherited is the ultimate genealogical insult in the hija' tradition.

titleEn: Your Father Is Your Father, and You Are His Son

verses.sadr: فما منك أعجب يا ابن استها ،

translationEn: Your father is your father, and you are his son — wretched is the son and wretched is the father. Your mother is a dark, branded slave-woman whose fingertips are like grasshoppers. Your father spends the night with her like a fox sneaking into a burrow. There is nothing more astonishing than you, O son of her backside — yet I am more astonished by those around you. When they hear foolishness, they respond to it — goats that bleat when struck. They consider a billy-goat among them to be like a horse — nay, the billy-goat among them is nobler. Do not summon them to clash with warriors — rather, call them to some disgrace to ride.
context: One of Hassan's most vicious satirical poems, using the full arsenal of hija' techniques: insulting both parents, comparing the enemy to animals (goats, foxes, grasshoppers), and declaring them unfit for battle. The repeated goat imagery reduces the enemy tribe to livestock, the most degrading comparison in Bedouin culture.

titleEn: You Boasted of the Banner — What Wretched Boasting

verses.ajuz: من الأم من يطا عفر التراب

translationEn: You boasted of the banner — what wretched boasting! A banner that was turned back to the right course. You placed your pride in it for a slave — the basest of those who tread the dust. You supposed — and the fool is one full of conjecture — but that is not sound judgment. That our encounter, when the day came at Mecca, would be your selling of red water-skins.
context: Hassan mocks the Quraysh for boasting about their war banner, pointing out that it was given to a slave — implying that no one of noble birth among them dared carry it. The reference to 'red water-skins' (a merchant's trade goods) ridicules them as merchants pretending to be warriors, a common insult against the trading Quraysh.

titleEn: Ask Quraysh and Their Allies

verses.sadr: وقد كان عهدي بها لم تنل

translationEn: Ask Quraysh and their allies: when was Awf ever counted among their lineage? Was there in the past a firm lineage that can be known, or is it a false claim that lies? Quraysh will expel you to a lineage more piercing than theirs — to the root-stock of a vile blacksmith, whose ancestor Urqub was tawny-skinned. To Taghlib — they are the worst of people, and you have no other refuge. In my time, Taghlib never attained any eminence or honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a false claim, a lying assertion
will expel you, will reject you

context: A genealogical satire questioning the claim of the Awf clan to Qurayshi lineage, tracing them instead to the Taghlib tribe and a blacksmith ancestor. In pre-Islamic Arab society, blacksmiths occupied a low social status. The poem exemplifies the genre of nasab-attacks (lineage challenges) at which Hassan excelled.

titleAr: ذكرت القروم الصيد من آل هاشم ،
titleEn: I Recalled the Noble Chiefs of the Hashim Clan

verses.sadr: ذكرت القروم الصيد من آل هاشم ،

translationEn: I recalled the noble chiefs, the mighty ones of the clan of Hashim — I am not one to achieve falsehood in what I say. Do you wonder that Hamza was struck down among them, a noble one? I have indeed called him noble. Did they not kill Amr, Utba and his son, Shayba, al-Hajjaj, and Ibn Habib — on the morning when al-Asi called out to Ali, who terrified him with a blow from a keen blade, drenched in blood?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the clan of Hashim (the Prophet's family)

context: A panegyric celebrating the Hashimite clan and their valor at Badr. Hassan lists the Quraysh chiefs killed by Muslim warriors and commemorates Ali ibn Abi Talib's famous duel, where he struck down al-Asi with a blood-drenched blade. The poem also elegizes Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle, known as the 'Lion of God,' who was martyred at Uhud.
